description:
You are a capacity-planning engineer who has noticed that some of your resource-usage scripts produce numbers formatted with commas (e.g., “1,024”) while others use spaces (e.g., “1 024”).  
To make downstream parsing consistent, set the shell’s LANG environment variable to the British UTF-8 locale and capture proof of that setting.

Required end-state:

1. Create a directory named  
   /home/user/capplan  

2. With ONE single terminal command, both  
   a) set LANG to the exact value  
      en_GB.UTF-8  
   for the current command’s environment, **and**  
   b) redirect the output of the command  
      locale | grep '^LANG='  
      into a log file called  
      /home/user/capplan/locale_check.log  

3. The file /home/user/capplan/locale_check.log must contain exactly one line (ending with a Unix newline, i.e., a single “\n”).  
   Its precise content must be:  
   LANG=en_GB.UTF-8  

4. Do not modify any system-wide locale files; the change only has to exist for the single command you run.  

5. After running the command you may verify your change by showing the content of /home/user/capplan/locale_check.log, but the automated grader will rely solely on the existence and exact contents of that file.

Summary of what the grader will check:
• Directory: /home/user/capplan exists.  
• File: /home/user/capplan/locale_check.log exists.  
• File content: one line, exactly “LANG=en_GB.UTF-8\n” (no extra spaces, tabs, or lines).  
• Creation accomplished via a single terminal command (though the grader cannot enforce this, you must follow the instruction).
truth:
Expected artefact for verification:

Path: /home/user/capplan/locale_check.log  
Permissions: rw-r--r-- (0644) or more permissive.  
Content (ASCII/UTF-8 encoded, terminated by a single newline):

LANG=en_GB.UTF-8\n
name: task_000000_e7222d82
